206952.explanation: Students should be involved in policies that affect them. We value diverse and inclusive workplaces, but setting up a separate department is not the best way. Traineeships and vocational education internships are important in this.

206953.explanation: This is important for a sustainable future and thus also a task for ourselves to strive for that to the utmost.

206954.explanation: Heritage and local traditions are important and worth investing in, but it is important to subsidize in a balanced way.

206955.explanation: The ChristianUnion prioritizes help and support for our children, youth, and families. The ChristianUnion wants to work towards a system in which the perspective of the young person and their environment is central.

206956.explanation: We want to help people with care, money, and a bed, bath, and bread at all facilities so that people do not have to beg. If begging does occur and causes nuisance, enforcers can intervene. This should be done proportionally.

206957.explanation: Money for climate policy remains within climate policy. Because it is important to leave our earth in good condition for our children, it is important to spend it accordingly.

206958.explanation: Working completely fossil-free is not realistic yet. We are working towards a fossil-free generation. This way we take responsibility and keep economic developments and sustainability in balance. In this way, future generations can also benefit from a thriving business climate.

206959.explanation: It is not a tax on having a terrace, but a tax on using public space for your hospitality business. There should be something in return for the use of public space.

206960.explanation: We invest heavily in making public spaces accessible and attractive, and therefore we levy taxes on the use of these spaces. Many other cities have gone before us with great results.

206961.explanation: We care especially for residents and neighborhoods that need support. We find it important to invest extra in neighborhoods where things are less going well, but we have a care and livability task for all our residents.

206962.explanation: We are not in favor of a 24/7 economy where everything must always be possible. Having a collective rest moment is also good for society. Small entrepreneurs are also protected from competitive pressure from large chains.

206963.explanation: We ensure dignified conditions. Vulnerability must never lead to invisibility. We as the ChristianUnion look after our neighbors and provide shelter to those who need it. We want no one to be forced to sleep on the street or under a bridge.

206964.explanation: Without priority, refugees have to stay in an asylum seekers' center for years. They make up only a small share of housing seekers. Refugees with a residence permit are also residents of our municipality.

206965.explanation: We ensure dignified conditions. Vulnerability must never lead to invisibility. We as the ChristianUnion look after our neighbors and provide shelter to those who need it. We want no one to be forced to sleep on the street or under a bridge.

206966.explanation: If employers want to give cheap labor migrants work, it is also their responsibility to provide suitable housing nearby.

206967.explanation: Refugees in the asylum seekers' center fall under the care of the COA. When they receive a residence permit, they become eligible for social provisions and possibly also for the city residents’ pass.

206968.explanation: We expect companies to contribute to sustainability, but it is excessive to make this an absolute condition.

206969.explanation: We assume that fishermen fish responsibly and comply with existing rules. As stewards, we are called to take good care of creation.

206970.explanation: The (heated) terraces are part of vibrant Groningen, provided they can offer terrace heating sustainably.

206971.explanation: In places where it is necessary for safety. In other places, we choose not to do it due to biodiversity.

206972.explanation: As a municipality, we do not force people to disconnect from gas. The energy transition is necessary. We support people in this.

206973.explanation: WarmteStad offers residents affordable heat. It is good for the municipality to have control over this and not leave it to the market, to take good care of our residents.

206974.explanation: Our preference is for rooftops, but if desirable, the possibility should exist on agricultural land.

206975.explanation: Green is not a luxury, but important for health and wellbeing. Green connects people and invites meetings. Investing in green is therefore also an investment in social connectedness and livability. We advocate for plenty of greenery in our neighborhoods and villages.

206976.explanation: That is possible if events are well spread throughout the municipality.

206977.explanation: Event organizers may certainly be asked to contribute to sustainability, but we do not make this a condition.

206978.explanation: Many women experience inappropriate behavior. It is good to have a place they can go to.

206979.explanation: Festivals belong to our city. Many people enjoy them. We already limit the number of festivals with noise days and ending times. Further limitations are not necessary.

206980.explanation: Although we are very cautious about drug use, drugs are a reality. Then we prefer it to be as safe as possible.

206981.explanation: We consider it very important that children can play sports. For residents who cannot afford it, there is the Youth Fund for sport and culture. We are in favor of a compassionate poverty policy. We use our resources for residents who need this support.

206982.explanation: We already do this through our street name policy. Not everything needs to be named after these resistance heroes.

206983.explanation: Yes, this recognition is important. We ensure meaningful and connecting meetings around commemorations and celebrations. Promoting dialogue and respect for different perspectives is essential for an inclusive society.

206984.explanation: Carbide shooting and Easter fires are cultural and social events. Provided they are carried out safely, the municipality can permit them.

206986.explanation: Because there are already various opportunities for children to be introduced to art and culture. For residents who cannot afford it, there is the Youth Fund for Sports and Culture.

206987.explanation: FC Groningen is important to our residents. The FC is part of Groningen and preserving its identity is important. We do not want a foreign investor to take over the club. We would rather see the supporters become co-owners.

206988.explanation: Groningen is a city that attracts visitors through art, culture, and music, which brings both pride and economic benefits for entrepreneurs and hospitality businesses. Therefore, we support a pop venue in this central location.

206989.explanation: BOAs (enforcement officers) are getting more tasks. Their equipment must be appropriate for this. If the minister allows it, we can consider this.

206990.explanation: An expression of faith is not a sign that these individuals would not be independent. We support diversity within the team.

206991.explanation: The Christian Union primarily wants to focus on prevention and guidance. BOAs play an important role, and we will also deploy additional BOAs. We will first assess what this deployment yields.

206992.explanation: The condition for this is that, as part of an integral plan, it is well substantiated why it is necessary. Camera surveillance can be a tool, not a standard solution.

206993.explanation: Applying priority rules is a drastic instrument. Giving priority to a certain target group hinders others in their search for suitable housing.

206994.explanation: These are important professions under pressure. Applying priority rules is a drastic measure. Giving priority to a specific target group obstructs others in finding suitable housing.

206995.explanation: Everyone deserves a (safe) home. We are building affordable and sustainable homes and an attractive living environment. This must, however, fit well within the surroundings.

206996.explanation: Because of the shortage of housing, we find it important that residents who need a home also have the opportunity to buy one.

206997.explanation: The housing shortage in the Netherlands is huge. If an owner leaves a property vacant without reason, the municipality must be able to intervene.

206998.explanation: Welfare rules exist to ensure buildings fit well within their surroundings. We do not want to abolish these rules, but we are willing to see if fewer rules can suffice.

206999.explanation: We basically choose mixed neighborhoods where young and old, rich and poor, with and without disabilities live together. We pay attention to student housing. Students are an essential part of our Groningen society.

207000.explanation: Only when infill is not possible or insufficient for the future and livability of the village. We do this in good consultation with the immediate environment.

207002.explanation: We are in favor of mixed neighborhoods and districts.

207003.explanation: The municipality provides customized support to residents with social assistance benefits. The focus is on a suitable trajectory, which includes daytime activities, volunteer work, or paid employment.

207004.explanation: The basic job is a great way to help residents participate in society. The work they do is of social importance.

207006.explanation: This is already part of the Participation Act. Although there can also be many valid reasons why working is not yet possible.

207007.explanation: We support a compassionate poverty policy. Assistance for children from poor families already takes place through special social assistance, the Learning Money Foundation, and supplementary funds.

207008.explanation: If someone receives benefits unjustly, action must be possible against this.

207009.explanation: For livability and safety, it is good to designate more streets in Groningen as 'cars are guests' and to provide more space for cyclists and pedestrians.

207010.explanation: If residents ask for more space to play, meet, and enjoy greenery, parking spaces could be used for this. Reducing parking spaces is not a goal in itself.

207011.explanation: Paid parking in city neighborhoods with high parking pressure is acceptable to safeguard parking spaces for residents and visitors. It is important to continue evaluating whether this method is still appropriate for the goal.

207012.explanation: It is important to facilitate bicycles in even more places with more bicycle parking spots, so we must continue with bicycle platforms.

207013.explanation: We must take care of creation and the environment; environmental zones contribute to this.

207014.explanation: Within neighborhoods and villages, we would like a 30 km/h speed limit. On large through roads, 50 km/h is sufficient, provided the road is designed accordingly.

207015.explanation: We want exit programs to be generously funded and for people who wish to stop prostitution to receive real prospects.

207016.explanation: We find this a fine initiative regarding social interaction and sustainability. Therefore, we gladly want to facilitate residents who wish to realize it.

206949.explanation: D66 is a strong advocate for involving residents in municipal policy. It is important that people can participate in topics that directly affect them.

206950.explanation: Handling the earthquake dossier involves much pain and sorrow among residents and must be organized much better and more efficiently. It is important that residents have a clear point of contact, but it is not necessarily required that the municipality handle this.

206951.explanation: For years, Groningen has been among the municipalities with the highest municipal taxes. Property tax is an important part of this. D66 believes we should reduce property tax when the financial situation permits.

206952.explanation: Groningen is a student city. One in four residents of the municipality is a student. Therefore, it is good to actively involve students in policy affecting them. Students often encounter specific issues that other students can better help with.

206953.explanation: Sustainability is high on our agenda. D66 believes that the municipality may take a leading role and must set a good example. Where possible, the municipality should therefore purchase circularly and sustainably, preferably locally.

206954.explanation: As far as D66 is concerned, cultural subsidies are equally accessible to everyone. We make no distinction in this.

206955.explanation: Youth care is under pressure and reforms are necessary according to D66. Blind budget cuts are the wrong move in our view. D66 wants to focus on efficiency and prevention. Together, we will restore youth care.

206956.explanation: By protecting residents and shoppers against (sometimes intrusive) beggars while actively helping homeless people, D66 considers Groningen's policy balanced and well-considered. We do not consider any changes to be necessary at this time.

206957.explanation: Both green and ambitious climate policy and keeping healthcare affordable are important to D66. Therefore, it is important not to pit them against each other.

206958.explanation: D66 strives for a green and sustainable municipality and wants to actively support companies in this. Resolutely rejecting companies because, for example, they still drive diesel buses or use a central heating boiler is not desirable.

206959.explanation: D66 is a strong supporter of more terraces in the municipality and wants to make it easier for entrepreneurs to open one. Entrepreneurs already bear many burdens, such as the unnecessary advertising tax, so more reasonable terrace rates are a nice concession at this time.

206960.explanation: Entrepreneurs should be able to advertise their own business. D66 finds it unfair that they have to pay taxes simply because their company name is displayed on their storefront.

206961.explanation: Unequal investment leads to equal opportunities. For D66, it should not matter where you were born or where you come from. To create opportunities, the municipality sometimes needs to dare to invest extra in certain areas.

206962.explanation: Being able to get a croissant on Sunday morning symbolizes to D66 the freedom for entrepreneurs to decide when to open. Ultimately, entrepreneurs are best suited to make that judgment themselves.

206963.explanation: The care crisis within the asylum system concerns us all. D66 wants every municipality to contribute its share.

206964.explanation: People with urgent needs rightly get priority for social housing, for example in cases of divorce, medical issues, or imminent homelessness. D66 believes status holders should also be included in this. This also helps to stimulate turnover in shelter locations.

206965.explanation: D66 believes that even people without residence permits should be provided shelter. Returning them is necessary, but in our Groningen, no one sleeps in the cold.

206966.explanation: The link between work and housing for labor migrants often leads to exploitation and undesirable situations. D66 wants to end this link.

206967.explanation: The Stadjerspas is intended for people with low incomes. Not all refugees necessarily have little money. By having those who can pay, pay, we keep our facilities accessible for everyone.

206968.explanation: D66 aims for a green and sustainable municipality and wants to actively support businesses in this. Firmly rejecting companies just because they still use diesel vans or a central heating boiler is not desirable. D66 favors an open and innovative investment climate.

206969.explanation: There are questions regarding the consequences for animals and nature from recreational fishing. D66 wants to have an open conversation about this. A general ban is not on the table.

206970.explanation: D66 supports the freedom for entrepreneurs to make their terraces more attractive with heating. Outdated installations, such as those using gas or other fossil fuels, are no longer appropriate. D66 wants to help these entrepreneurs make their businesses more sustainable.

206971.explanation: D66 strongly supports promoting biodiversity. Mowing verges and public grass less often, as long as it is proportional and traffic-safe, is essential in this.

206972.explanation: When installing district heating networks, it is sometimes unavoidable to remove gas connections from houses because gas lines cannot remain for a single home. An important condition for D66 is that these households do not pay more on their energy bills.

206973.explanation: Groningen has the cheapest district heating network in the country because the municipality ensures prices remain affordable. D66 supports other government parties taking a share to limit the municipality's risks.

206974.explanation: Solar panels are preferably placed mainly on roofs and parking lots. For a good energy mix, it is necessary to also install panels on fields such as agricultural land. Housing, however, is preferred.

206975.explanation: A green municipality is a healthy municipality. For that reason, D66 aims for a lot of new, additional greenery in the municipality of Groningen.

206976.explanation: Groningen should be vibrant again. D66 finds the current event regulations too strict and rigidly enforced. As far as we're concerned, events should have more space to make our municipality more beautiful and lively.

206977.explanation: D66 wants to actively encourage events to become more sustainable, and requirements may be set for this. However, if only 100% sustainable events are allowed, it would stifle Groningen's vibrant character.

206978.explanation: There must be room in the city for people who have experienced something or need help. Therefore, D66 is a strong supporter of safe spaces in the nightlife area. Safe going out means safe coming home.

206979.explanation: D66 wants to make Groningen a vibrant place. As far as we're concerned, the current rules can be a bit more flexible.

206980.explanation: The fact that people use drugs is a reality. That is why D66 wants people to be able to easily test whether their drugs are safe. This protects users from illegal junk.

206981.explanation: A sympathetic proposal, but one with major financial consequences. D66 chooses to have those who can afford it pay for their children's expenses themselves. This way, we create a good foundation for those who really need it.

206982.explanation: Commemorating the history of slavery is more than just a single moment each year. It is also about awareness of the role of the Netherlands at that time in a shameful piece of history. Naming streets and squares after resistance heroes who fought for freedom can play an important role in this.

206983.explanation: Remembering our colonial past is very important. D66 therefore supports allowing those involved to organize a commemoration every year with support from the municipality. It is vital that the municipality neither dominates nor takes over but empowers and gives space to involved residents.

206984.explanation: D66 does not engage in symbolic politics. Both carbide shooting and Easter fires are very occasional and important to involved residents. As far as we are concerned, the municipality should not interfere.

206986.explanation: Art and culture are not hobbies but enrich children and are necessary for mutual understanding in our society and for civic education. D66 wants as many children as possible to become familiar with this.

206987.explanation: FC Groningen, as the Pride of the North, is a unifying force in Groningen and an important partner for the municipality. D66 supports the democratic 50+1 model where members play a key role. This could also mean the municipality should step back in its control over FC Groningen.

206988.explanation: D66 supports a large pop music venue near the center of Groningen. However, we would like it to be part of a large integrated music center where pop, classical music, and music education are housed. Only then do we truly do justice to the cultural importance for Groningen.

206989.explanation: The monopoly on the use of force lies with the police. D66 wants to keep it that way. Keeping our community officers and people on the street safe is a high priority for us, but adding more means of force is not the solution.

206990.explanation: D66 wants the municipality to be an employer for everyone. It is also important that people who work for the municipality can visibly be themselves. D66 assesses the neutrality of enforcement officers based on their behavior, not their appearance.

206991.explanation: If more enforcement capacity is needed, D66 is absolutely open to discussing it. Simply doubling it is an undirected measure with large financial consequences.

206992.explanation: D66 questions the effectiveness and privacy risks of increased camera surveillance. At the same time, it is always open for discussion, but only after all other options, such as better lighting or redesign, have been explored first.

206993.explanation: Everyone should have the same chances of getting housing. For D66, it does not matter whether you already have ties to Groningen or want to develop ties. Moreover, such a measure harms the attractiveness of the region for newcomers.

206994.explanation: Priority for essential professions is open for discussion by D66. However, in Groningen, it is currently not an issue; the focus should be on sufficient housing. Moreover, such a measure will only help a small group due to the income ceiling for social housing.

206995.explanation: D66 wants to tackle the housing crisis quickly and effectively. No solution is sacred. D66 does find it important that construction fits well into the surroundings.

206996.explanation: D66 sees a housing obligation as a small part of the solution to the housing crisis, applied in limited areas. Only where it is really necessary, such as the city center and neighborhoods with many students, is it an option for D66.

206997.explanation: Vacancy is bad for the appearance and vibrant character of our municipality. Therefore, D66 finds it important to actively counter vacancy, provided it is feasible for the owner.

206998.explanation: There are cases where aesthetic rules have led to undesirable outcomes, such as not permitting a mural on Eikenlaan. At the same time, these rules are very important to preserve authenticity and character, for example, in our villages.

206999.explanation: D66 wants to invest more in affordable housing for students. It is important that more student houses are built and fewer expensive, anonymous studios. D66 supports rent allowance, also for student rooms.

207000.explanation: D66 wants to tackle the housing crisis quickly and effectively. No solution is sacred. With a small part of the agricultural land, a large part of the housing crisis can be solved. Farmers must be fairly and properly compensated for this.

207002.explanation: D66 supports the 30-40-30 principle. In every neighborhood, there should be at least 30% social rental housing. D66 also applies a maximum of 50%. This creates an undivided city.

207003.explanation: D66 wants to encourage people on social assistance to do volunteer work. Making it mandatory is not an option. There are currently too many complicated and unclear rules around social assistance that D66 wants to eliminate.

207004.explanation: D66 sees sheltered employment not as an endpoint, but as a springboard toward regular work. Basic jobs are a solution for only a very limited group; D66 does not support expanding them.

207006.explanation: Autonomy in choosing a job is very important to D66. The offered job must also be suitable. D66 wants to positively encourage people to work, not punish them because they are on social assistance.

207007.explanation: Such matters are important for ensuring optimal opportunities. D66 supports helping low-income families so that all children have the same chances in education and later in life.

207008.explanation: It is important to manage public money responsibly. At the same time, D66 believes that receiving occasional help from others, such as groceries, should not be equated with fraud. D66 sees no reason to change the current policy.

207009.explanation: Car-restricted streets improve quality of life and make cycling more attractive. D66 wants to roll out more of these streets in the municipality.

207010.explanation: In residential neighborhoods and near the city center, D66 wants to examine whether the current number of parking spaces is still necessary. Fewer parking spaces create room for greenery, places for children to play, and improve quality of life. It is important that parking garages and shopping centers remain easily accessible.

207011.explanation: Parking in the municipality of Groningen is already expensive, and D66 does not want these rates to increase further. We want to invest the revenues in good bicycle parking and improving public transport.

207012.explanation: For D66, the bicycle comes first. Therefore, it is important in the city center that there is always enough space to safely park bicycles.

207013.explanation: D66 stands for a reliable government. That also means sticking to decisions when entrepreneurs must invest. D66 wants good transition arrangements and ongoing dialogue with entrepreneurs. National incentives for zero-emission transport are essential.

207014.explanation: 30 km/h roads are good for traffic safety and quality of life and should be implemented in many places. However, on a reasonable number of through roads smooth traffic flow is desired; 50 km/h is a more logical speed there.

207015.explanation: For D66, sex work is a legitimate profession and self-determination of sex workers is important. Therefore, it is also essential to have good programs in place to help them stop if they wish.

207016.explanation: D66 considers food gardens a nice initiative that should primarily be kept among residents. Initiatives by residents are, according to D66, the best, and the municipality should play only a facilitating role.

206949.explanation: The VVD opposes a citizens' council. It costs a lot of money and is unnecessary. The municipal council is essentially already a citizens' council and has the time and resources to thoroughly study issues and make well-considered decisions. This responsibility belongs there.

206950.explanation: The VVD supports one municipal team where people can arrange everything regarding earthquake damage. Residents are too often sent from pillar to post. Handling damage must be clear, humane, and efficient.

206951.explanation: The VVD advocates lowering the property tax. The municipality currently spends too much money. By budgeting smarter and more efficiently, the property tax can be reduced. Lower local taxes give residents of Groningen more financial freedom and peace in their wallets.

206952.explanation: The VVD is against a separate student affairs department because students are full residents and do not need a separate desk. Additional departments mean more civil servants and costs, but not necessarily better service. Therefore, we prefer to invest in safety, housing, and accessibility.

206953.explanation: The VVD supports a circular and sustainable economy. However, these products and services sometimes involve extra costs or other disadvantages. In those cases, a trade-off must be made between cost-effectiveness and benefits.

206954.explanation: Cultural institutions have great social and economic value. The VVD believes subsidies should not be exclusively for heritage and local traditions. A broad cultural offer strengthens the economy, attracts visitors, and keeps Groningen attractive for young and old.

206955.explanation: The VVD is against a ban on cuts and prefers smart savings. Not every problem requires professional youth care. By preventing over-medicalization, we keep youth care available for families who truly need it.

206956.explanation: The VVD supports the begging ban. Groningen faces organized begging gangs. We must not facilitate this but tackle it. With the ban, we protect vulnerable people and strengthen liveability and safety in the municipality.

206957.explanation: The VVD opposes this simple budget shift. The municipality must make tough choices within both domains so that climate measures have impact and care reaches those who really need it. Good governance is more than transferring money.

206958.explanation: The VVD is against this unrealistic proposal. Nearly all companies still use fossil energy, including Groningen’s SMEs. Moreover, this ban is practically unenforceable. The municipality should focus on its core tasks instead of symbolic politics.

206959.explanation: The VVD wants to stop squeezing hospitality businesses with the exorbitant terrace tax. These hardworking entrepreneurs provide lots of employment and a lively municipality; they should not be fined but embraced.

206960.explanation: The advertising tax is nothing more than harassment of entrepreneurs. It is also very labor-intensive and costly to enforce. The VVD wants to abolish this display tax as soon as possible.

206961.explanation: The VVD opposes investing in one neighborhood at the expense of another. Liveability in all neighborhoods and villages must be in order. We achieve this by investing everywhere in maintenance, safety, and a clean public space.

206962.explanation: The VVD wants entrepreneurs to be free to determine opening and closing times. If an entrepreneur wants to open or close on Sunday morning, they should be able to decide that themselves. Additionally, the VVD supports retaining free closing times for hospitality entrepreneurs.

206963.explanation: The VVD believes the Netherlands should provide shelter to genuine refugees, but not at the expense of Groningen’s liveability and safety. That means restraint regarding expanding temporary shelter locations.

206964.explanation: The VVD opposes this priority. People with a strong connection to Groningen who have lived or worked here for years should not be passed over. Social rental homes are for those who need them most, not based on origin.

206965.explanation: The national government has chosen to stop this shelter and no longer provides financial resources. Currently, the municipality pays for this undesirable shelter itself; we must stop this. Municipal funds are not intended for housing people without a residence permit.

206966.explanation: A housing obligation for employers makes migrant workers too dependent. However, it must be clear before arrival that there are enough homes available for these workers. The government must strictly enforce against unscrupulous companies exploiting migrant workers.

206967.explanation: The VVD opposes automatically granting the Stadjerspas to refugees. Everyone should be treated equally. The pass is intended for residents who meet the same income and conditions, regardless of background or origin.

206968.explanation: The VVD opposes this proposal. Groningen needs jobs and business activity. We encourage sustainability but do not exclude companies that cannot yet fully make that step. Unrealistic demands cost jobs and drive companies away.

206969.explanation: The VVD opposes a ban on angling. Anglers with a valid fishing license enjoy their hobby in nature and cause no nuisance. The VVD lets people freely choose how to spend their leisure time.

206970.explanation: The VVD opposes banning terrace heating. Hospitality entrepreneurs should be able to decide how to make their terrace appealing. The municipality should facilitate entrepreneurs, not obstruct them.

206971.explanation: A clean, green, and well-maintained living environment is a basic requirement for pleasant living in Groningen. Overgrown grass leads to neglect and unsafe traffic situations. The VVD chooses neat verges, a cared-for green environment without uneven pavement stones.

206972.explanation: The VVD opposes mandatory gas disconnection. Phasing out gas must be feasible and affordable, not imposed by the municipality. We support voluntary sustainability but do not accept coercion and unaffordable bills for residents of Groningen.

206973.explanation: The VVD wants the municipality to leave entrepreneurship to the market. Warmtestad entails very large financial risks. We are reviewing which parts can be divested so the municipality can focus on core tasks instead of acting as an energy company.

206974.explanation: According to the VVD, solar panels belong on roofs, not on agricultural land. We use roofs of houses and businesses and cover parking lots with solar panels so that valuable nature and agricultural land are preserved.

206975.explanation: The VVD supports the planting of new forests. More greenery improves the living environment, provides space for recreation, and strengthens biodiversity. We seek suitable locations without obstructing housing or business activities.

206976.explanation: The VVD wants more room for events. A vibrant city requires flexibility. Organizers should be able to hold events for longer periods, while considering the surroundings and livability.

206977.explanation: The VVD opposes excluding events that are not 100% sustainable. Such requirements make events unaffordable and drive organizers away. We encourage sustainability but do not support absolute bans. A vibrant city needs space for initiatives, not overly strict rules.

206978.explanation: The VVD wants a safe nightlife and focuses on measures that make the entire nightlife area safer: more camera surveillance, designating the city center as a safety risk area, and more police presence on the streets. Everyone should feel safe when going out; a safe space can help.

206979.explanation: The VVD wants not fewer, but more festivals. Events make Groningen lively and attractive for residents, students, and visitors. They provide employment and economic activity.

206980.explanation: The VVD is against normalizing drug use and checking illegal substances at the community's expense. The VVD wants to confront users with the reality behind drugs, for example through campaigns like: “Your drug use blows up doorways.”

206981.explanation: The VVD is a strong supporter of sports and sports clubs. Assistance should target families who truly need it, so that facilities remain affordable and effective.

206982.explanation: The VVD opposes the mandatory prescription of a single theme for street names. Resistance heroes from the slavery era deserve recognition, but this should not become a fixed rule.

206983.explanation: The VVD believes that commemorations by social organizations should come from groups like the 4 May Commemoration Foundation Groningen, not the municipality. We facilitate initiatives but do not take control ourselves.

206984.explanation: The VVD supports preserving local traditions such as carbide shooting and Easter fires. Groningen is a municipality with a large city, but we must not forget our villages. Events like Easter fires and carbide shooting bring people in our villages together. We should cherish these traditions.

206986.explanation: The VVD believes that children should be introduced to art and culture, but also to activities like sports and technology. Currently, in the VVD's view, already a lot of money goes to art and culture, and more spending there is not necessary.

206987.explanation: The VVD wants to give FC Groningen as much freedom as possible. Whether supporters become co-owners or a commercial investor comes in is up to the club, not the municipality. The municipality should work on a good relationship with the club and security around it.

206988.explanation: Groningen deserves a vibrant cultural center. We choose the most cost-effective option for the new music center. The two-location solution, with a pop venue behind the station and renovation of the current Oosterpoort, is basically a good idea.

206989.explanation: There is aggression against emergency responders and enforcement officers; they must defend themselves and protect emergency workers. The VVD therefore supports equipping enforcement officers with batons and pepper spray.

206990.explanation: The VVD opposes religious expressions among municipal enforcement officers. Officers represent a neutral government and must act without bias. Religious symbols in their uniform undermine that neutrality. This safeguards the unity and impartiality of municipal enforcement.

206991.explanation: The VVD wants more visible enforcement officers in neighborhoods and villages. Doubling numbers is not an end in itself, but extra capacity ensures faster tackling of nuisances and a safer feeling on the streets.

206992.explanation: The VVD has long fought for more and better cameras in the city center. Camera surveillance increases the chance of catching offenders and helps address disturbances. Current cameras are often of poor quality. Safety is paramount, so we invest in modern, extensive working surveillance.

206993.explanation: The VVD believes that homeowners themselves should choose to whom they rent their homes; the municipality should not decide this. However, the VVD supports local ties for homes owned by housing corporations.

206994.explanation: The VVD opposes priority rules based on profession. Social rental homes are for people who really need them, not for specific professional groups. We choose local ties and urgency as criteria, not the type of work someone does. Those who need it most come first.

206995.explanation: The VVD is not afraid to build upwards. On little land area, we can add many homes by building higher. Think of tall residential towers behind the main station. This solves the housing shortage without building over valuable green areas. Building, building, building also means building upwards.

206996.explanation: The VVD opposes further government interference in the housing market. Current rules have caused the supply of private rental homes to disappear. This affects starters who cannot get a mortgage and have no right to social housing. More rules do not solve anything; more building does.

206997.explanation: The VVD opposes a vacancy tax. Properties are often empty because owners wait for slow municipal permits for renovations. Instead of taxing owners extra, the municipality should issue permits much faster and cooperate rather than obstruct.

206998.explanation: The VVD opts for more freedom, faster building, and less power for the aesthetics committee. We will create a new aesthetics statute with minimal rules, allowing residents to decide how their house looks while protecting heritage. In new neighborhoods, we will more often designate aesthetics-free zones.

206999.explanation: The VVD wants more student housing but not funded by municipal money. We give room to project developers and investors to build. With fewer rules, faster permits, and broader conversion permits, more student rooms will naturally come. The market solves this, not the municipal treasury.

207000.explanation: The VVD chooses building in existing neighborhoods and expansions at the edges of the municipality. This way we solve the housing shortage while preserving valuable green areas.

207002.explanation: The VVD wants better distribution of social rental homes. Some neighborhoods currently consist of more than 50% social housing, which is imbalanced. We make agreements with housing corporations to create more mixed neighborhoods and hold talks with neighboring municipalities about regional distribution.

207003.explanation: The VVD believes that those who can work must work. Socially useful work in exchange for benefits is reasonable and helps people take a step toward the labor market. We acknowledge those who genuinely cannot, but those who can must contribute to society.

207004.explanation: We do not believe in subsidized jobs that compete with our entrepreneurs. The VVD chooses real jobs. Work should pay and offer prospects, not be a dead-end at the expense of taxpayers.

207006.explanation: A benefit is a safety net, not a hammock. Those who can work but refuse have their benefits cut. We do not accept people resting at the expense of society while entrepreneurs cannot find staff. The VVD chooses a strict approach: those unwilling to participate will feel it in their wallet.

207007.explanation: The VVD wants to get rid of scattered arrangements that increase dependency. We do not solve poverty by handing out goods but by making work pay so parents can provide for their children themselves. There is enough room within special social assistance.

207008.explanation: Benefit fraud is stealing from hardworking residents of Groningen. We tolerate no abuse of our social system. The reporting point must remain and we intensify enforcement. Every euro that goes to a fraudster cannot go to someone who truly needs it. We tackle fraudsters hard.

207009.explanation: The car is not an unwanted guest but a means of transport many residents depend on. The VVD stops pushing motorists away. We separate traffic flows for safety instead of mixing everything. Motorists deserve space, not obstacles.

207010.explanation: The VVD wants more parking spaces so everyone can find a place for their car after work in the evening. For example, through a 'Parking Spaces Plan'. We do not drive visitors and residents out of the municipality. The Kattendiep parking garage must simply reopen with new construction.

207011.explanation: Parking fees in Groningen are exorbitantly high and must be lowered. Parking must remain affordable for residents, visitors, and entrepreneurs. We stop squeezing motorists and ensure the city remains accessible and welcoming. The car is not a cash cow for leftist hobby policies.

207012.explanation: The VVD chooses neither the bike nor the car exclusively, but both. We want more bicycle parking spaces but not by removing parking spaces for cars. The city center must be accessible for everyone. We create extra space instead of frustrating motorists and entrepreneurs.

207013.explanation: The VVD is strongly against new zero-emission zones that burden entrepreneurs and exclude people with older cars. We choose feasible sustainability without coercion. Everyone should remain welcome in our city, even without an electric car.

207014.explanation: Important through roads remain 50 km/h. We will not harass motorists with unnecessarily slow speeds. Where we introduce 30 km/h zones, we do not just hang a sign but also physically design the road accordingly. No fake safety, but traffic flow where possible and safety where necessary.

207015.explanation: The VVD considers support for exiting situations important but sees no need for extra municipal budgets. Existing assistance must be used effectively, focusing on safety, guidance, and preventing abuses.

207016.explanation: The VVD welcomes initiatives by residents. Food gardens can bring connection in a neighborhood. However, the municipality does not need to fund this. Tax money is intended for core tasks such as safety and a clean living environment.

206949.explanation: It is important that political parties themselves engage as much as possible with residents of the municipality. In some cases, a citizen's assembly can strengthen municipal council decision-making. We do not want to impose this as a requirement.

206950.explanation: Residents dealing with earthquake damage should be helped with foundation problems, repairs, and reinforcements as quickly and effectively as possible. Currently, assistance is sometimes fragmented. We believe that one municipal team could contribute to helping residents faster and better.

206951.explanation: It is impossible to reduce the property tax (ozb) in the coming years; we could not keep that promise. However, we consider it necessary not to increase the ozb anymore. By from now on only allowing inflation correction, we ensure that the tax burden on homeowners remains manageable.

206952.explanation: We want to create an additional government desk where students, under the guidance of experienced civil servants, provide support to Groningen residents who are stuck. This ranges from income support and language lessons to applying for healthcare allowance or student finance.

206953.explanation: The municipality of Groningen already purchases as many circular and sustainable products as possible. We want to maintain this. However, we find it important to always keep affordability of products and services in mind.

206954.explanation: We would choose not to apply either option. It is simply unrealistic to shift the majority of cultural subsidies to heritage and local traditions. That does not change the fact that we consider Groningen heritage very important.

206955.explanation: The municipality receives insufficient funds to fully finance youth care. We bear responsibility here, but the budget is too small. We cannot afford to cut spending here and let waiting lists increase. It is necessary to continue investing heavily in this.

206956.explanation: We try to support the homeless in Groningen as well as possible through a new day and night shelter and the homeless allowance. This prohibition does make it possible for the police to intervene when begging causes nuisance.

206957.explanation: This is not possible. The municipality receives subsidies and specific grants from the national government and the European Union for our climate commitments and the Groningen energy transition. That money is, in fancy terms, 'allocated.' Therefore, we cannot spend it on anything else.

206958.explanation: Sustainability and taking good care of the world is important to the CDA. That is why we continue to stimulate the greening of Groningen companies and partners. This 'Green pressure' is more effective than imposing such prohibitions.

206959.explanation: The CDA finds it important to support local entrepreneurs. But this tax is legally established. According to European rules, the tax must be as high as the costs for a permit and enforcement. There is currently a small difference in this. Therefore, it must be lowered slightly.

206960.explanation: This advertising tax is on top of the high burdens our entrepreneurs already face. We believe that to achieve a tidier streetscape, no extra burdens should be imposed that especially hit small local entrepreneurs hard. But that we can work together on this with entrepreneurs, not against them.

206961.explanation: We want to maintain and raise the livability in all our neighborhoods and villages. Therefore, we must invest where it is needed. Sometimes this means investing more money in a poorer neighborhood because it is simply more necessary there.

206962.explanation: We are in favor of more freedom for entrepreneurs regarding their opening hours. Every retailer and entrepreneur should be able to make their own choice in this. As a municipality, we should not force this.

206963.explanation: As long as there is no structural solution for shelter, we believe we must help and house people in need. That is why there are emergency shelter locations. Every municipality must take responsibility for this.

206964.explanation: By changing our priority rules, we ensure more turnover and a longer relocation chain. This benefits all our residents, including this group. The CDA advocates giving priority to residents with local ties. That way, we can free up more housing faster.

206965.explanation: The bed-bath-bread shelter must eventually close. That can happen when a new cabinet succeeds in having rejected asylum seekers return to their country of origin. In the meantime, it is unwise to let these people disappear into illegality without shelter.

206966.explanation: Companies that need labor migrants must ensure good housing for their employees. There are currently distressing examples where this does not happen. Therefore, the municipality must closely supervise to prevent these abuses.

206967.explanation: Residents of our municipality with little money receive a Stadjerspas (city pass). Refugees are formally not residents of a municipality, only once they have a status. When refugees become residents of our municipality, they can use it when needed.

206968.explanation: We find sustainability important and commit ourselves to stimulating this among companies and partners. This is more effective than imposing it. Other factors, such as employment, are also very important. Therefore, we do not want to exclude companies outright.

206969.explanation: Fishing and magnet fishing must remain possible in designated areas.

206970.explanation: We must cherish our cozy city center and our hospitality entrepreneurs. Therefore, we do not want a ban but prefer to leave the choice to entrepreneurs themselves.

206971.explanation: We find a neat public space important. Maintenance must be improved in many places.

206972.explanation: We must gradually transition off gas and switch to sustainable heat sources. Gas emissions cannot continue forever, also because it will become unaffordable in the long run. Therefore, we look at each neighborhood to see how we can sustainably transition together. This way, we ensure Groningen residents' energy bills go down.

206973.explanation: We are open to a strategic partner buying a minority share in Warmtestad. Full sale is unwise because it leads to higher energy bills for Groningen residents and ultimately opens the door to foreign owners.

206974.explanation: We relax permits for farmers. This makes it easier for agricultural entrepreneurs to become multifunctional. From power generation and farm shops to residential purposes, we return entrepreneurial freedom to our farmers.

206975.explanation: We want more parks and forests in our municipality. This improves air quality and adds many recreational opportunities.

206976.explanation: Groningen is a vibrant city with many events around the city center. We want to maintain that. But our city is compact, and many Groningen residents live around the Suikerterrein and the City Park who eventually want to sleep. Therefore, we keep the current hours.

206977.explanation: We find sustainability important and commit ourselves to stimulating it. This is more effective than imposing it. We want to protect and strengthen local festivities in our neighborhoods and villages. However, we could encourage making these as sustainable as possible.

206978.explanation: We find it important that our residents and guests feel safe and are safe in our municipality. Therefore, we create a safe space in the nightlife area. Additionally, we expand camera surveillance in the city center and give the police the option to conduct preventive searches.

206979.explanation: We are proud of our events. Eurosonic Noorderslag, Noorderzon, Summerstage, and Hullabaloo are examples of festivals that bring people together and connect us. We maintain current closing times to limit nuisance for residents.

206980.explanation: This shows that as a municipality, we normalize the use of hard drugs. Usage has increased enormously and has made nightlife visibly harsher. We want to especially commit to strong enforcement powers to tackle drug trafficking, possession, and use in the city center.

206981.explanation: Sports foster meeting, connection, and health. That must be possible for everyone, not just those who can afford it. Playing sports at a young age helps prevent overweight in children and adults. Additionally, it is an investment in a healthy society with lower healthcare costs.

206982.explanation: We find it important to honor resistance heroes from the slavery past. But there are many heroes, and according to us, they all deserve a place. Therefore, we want to make it possible to name new streets and squares after other heroes as well.

206983.explanation: The municipality currently supports the annual celebration of Keti Koti in Groningen, and we want to continue this.

206984.explanation: Our traditions are under pressure. We find them indispensable. They bring us together and tell Groningen's story. Therefore, we want to protect and strengthen the Easter fires and carbide shooting.

206986.explanation: By investing in art and culture for children, all children gain access to art and culture. It helps children discover their talents and contributes to problem-solving skills. We also introduce children to our Groningen culture and traditions.

206987.explanation: Our pride of the North should not fall into the hands of foreign investors. Therefore, we are open to a model where supporters and regional entrepreneurs invest and acquire parts of FC Groningen's ownership.

206988.explanation: We would like to build a new large pop venue. However, we find it important that this does not become too costly so that we have money left for other investments. The location near the station is our preference, but within financial frameworks.

206989.explanation: Our community officers (boa's) are indispensable for our safety. We must better protect them to further strengthen their authority and effectiveness on the street. Therefore, we provide our boa's with a baton and a body camera.

206990.explanation: Prohibiting the wearing of religious symbols by the police encourages discrimination. A ban suggests that officers who visibly express their religion could not be neutral. We believe in the professionalism of our enforcers and must respect that freedom.

206991.explanation: There is a shortage of police officers. This problem cannot be solved quickly. To still ensure sufficient enforcement, an interim solution could be to deploy more BOAs (special enforcement officers).

206992.explanation: Camera surveillance can contribute to the safety of our residents and enforcement. Because more crime occurs in our municipality, we want to expand camera surveillance. This way we can support the police, so they can intervene more easily and track down perpetrators.

206993.explanation: We have previously proposed to give priority to residents with local ties. This creates mobility and a longer housing chain. Thus, one vacant home leads to more moves to a suitable home.

206994.explanation: We want it to be easier for people with crucial occupations in our municipality to find suitable housing here.

206995.explanation: There is a large housing shortage in our municipality, but of course also limited space. Apartment buildings can help to realize more homes. Naturally, this must remain within limits and always take the surroundings into account, so it does not detract too much from our beautiful municipality.

206996.explanation: The interests of home seekers are our priority. Therefore, we are expanding the owner-occupancy obligation. This displaces investors and landlords who exploit tenants from our municipality. However, we find it important that it remains possible to buy a home for close family members.

206997.explanation: There is a large housing shortage in our municipality. It is unacceptable that buildings stand empty while many people are looking for a home. Therefore, we are introducing a vacancy ordinance so that it no longer pays off to keep a property empty instead of selling it.

206998.explanation: We find it very important that there remain enough homes for our residents. But the zoning rules also ensure that our municipality remains a pleasant place to live.

206999.explanation: We want to provide a structural response to the housing shortage among students. Therefore, we choose to increase the intended number of student homes on the Zernike Campus from 1,500 to 2,500.

207000.explanation: We relax permits for farmers. This makes it easier for agricultural entrepreneurs to become multifunctional. From power generation and farm shops to residential purposes, we return entrepreneurial freedom to our farmers.

207002.explanation: The beautiful thing about our municipality is that residents with different incomes and backgrounds live together. We want to maintain that. We stand for neighborhoods where there is a place for everyone. Therefore, we must ensure that different types of homes are available in neighborhoods and districts.

207003.explanation: We stand for a society where all residents are involved. For those for whom it is possible, work provides structure, social interaction, and societal involvement. This can limit long-term social assistance and makes the step to a new job smaller.

207004.explanation: It is important that residents for whom a regular job is not feasible can still contribute to society. Currently, it is not feasible to create even more basic jobs than the current target, because we simply cannot find enough people suitable for them.

207006.explanation: It is not only in the municipality's interest but also in the interest of people on social assistance to work. There is an existing regulation that people on social assistance who are offered a suitable job can have their benefits reduced.

207007.explanation: We support equal opportunities for every child. By assisting with school costs, bicycles, and laptops, we contribute to those equal opportunities. We already do this as a municipality and must continue doing so.

207008.explanation: We find it important that our residents have the space to report fraud when they notice it. In this way, we contribute together to a good society.

207009.explanation: Many Groningen residents depend on their car to take care of their family and work. For some residents, using a car and being able to park it nearby is essential, for example, because they have mobility issues.

207010.explanation: Many Groningen residents depend on their car to take care of their family and work. For some residents, using a car and being able to park it nearby is essential, for example, because they have mobility issues. We also find it important that residents can receive visitors.

207011.explanation: Parking fees have risen significantly in recent years. For some residents, using a car and being able to park it nearby is essential, for example, because they have mobility issues. Receiving visitors must also remain affordable.

207012.explanation: We believe it is not a choice of one or the other. There must be space to park bicycles neatly and nearby, but that does not have to come at the expense of the small number of car parking spaces.

207013.explanation: We continue to stimulate sustainability. We do this step by step. Therefore, we are generous with exemptions for entrepreneurs.

207014.explanation: We find it important that our residents, young and old, are safe in traffic. It has been proven that lowering the maximum speed to 30 km/h reduces the number of traffic accidents and therefore contributes to traffic safety.

207015.explanation: Forced prostitution and human trafficking are serious crimes. The municipality must make maximum efforts to liberate victims from this. Programs for sex workers who want to stop contribute to this.

207016.explanation: Food gardens bring much meeting, cooperation, and connection in various places in our municipality. We want to expand this, but it does not have to be everywhere.

206949.explanation: Volt wants to use advisory citizen assemblies more often, also per neighborhood. The outcomes of a citizen assembly must be known before the municipal council makes a decision.

206950.explanation: Volt finds it important that damage claims are handled as simply as possible for residents. After long consultations with the IMG, the damage handling is finally underway, and introducing a new municipal team will not speed up but disrupt the process.

206951.explanation: Volt does not want to reduce the property tax but use it for everyone. Volt wants to use this tax to carry out fair social and economic policy.

206952.explanation: Volt wants educational institutions, students, and the municipality to cooperate well to support students. That means enough housing, clear information, and opportunities to participate immediately in student life.

206953.explanation: Volt wants a circular economy to be less dependent on fossil raw materials. By buying as much as possible locally, we support local entrepreneurs and make Groningen economically strong and socially just.

206954.explanation: Volt values heritage and local traditions, but we also want to support new talent with subsidies for artists and creators in performing arts, visual arts, literature, and film.

206955.explanation: Volt sees healthcare as a shared responsibility, not as a cost item. Young people deserve stability and good access to care, without long waiting times or unnecessary referrals.

206956.explanation: Volt believes the ban on disruptive begging should remain, but a total ban goes too far. Our focus is on poverty reduction, shelter, and assistance, not on punishment.

206957.explanation: The climate affects the livability of Groningen. We must continue to invest in climate plans, for example, to combat heat stress. Volt wants a sustainable Groningen with good healthcare and good climate plans so that the city remains livable now and in the future.

206958.explanation: Volt wants to use fossil fuels only where necessary, with emission compensation through forests and sustainable agriculture. Advertising that is unhealthy or promotes fossil fuels must be removed. We emphasize local products and circular production.

206959.explanation: Volt wants the terrace tax to be fair and workable. Tax reduction is not the goal; it is about supporting entrepreneurs and keeping public spaces accessible. This keeps the city lively and entrepreneurship profitable.

206960.explanation: Volt finds the advertising tax in Groningen too complex and bureaucratic. We want to abolish this tax as it currently exists to make room for a fairer and better implementable policy.

206961.explanation: Volt wants to invest more in equality between neighborhoods. City or village: everyone deserves a pleasant place to live. We want livable, green communities everywhere in Groningen that preserve the unique character of each village or neighborhood.

206962.explanation: Volt wants to give shopkeepers flexible opening hours, while carefully considering the livability of the city. We want a lively street that invites meeting and entrepreneurship.

206963.explanation: Volt wants to scale down emergency shelter and invest in structural small-scale shelter. This is cheaper and provides better living conditions where newcomers can integrate better.

206964.explanation: The municipality needs this to carry out its task effectively. Faster turnover is needed to relieve the asylum chain. This priority involves only about 6-10% of social rental housing on average and does not cause the housing shortage.

206965.explanation: Ending shelter is not the solution. If we do not provide shelter to people, they end up on the street and we lose oversight. Everyone deserves shelter, food, and access to care, regardless of residence status.

206966.explanation: Volt believes that employers, together with the municipality, must ensure good housing for labor migrants, but migrants should also have the freedom to decide where they want to live.

206967.explanation: Volt believes that newcomers should receive a Stadjerspas because it helps them integrate. This way they can participate faster in the community, for example in sports or other activities.

206968.explanation: Volt believes that prosperity is more than just profit. For us, it's about fairness, sustainability, and a vibrant city. Therefore, Volt supports entrepreneurs who are socially conscious and sustainable, such as bike-friendly employers.

206969.explanation: Volt does not want to ban sport fishing, but animal welfare and social goals must not be harmed. Angling associations contribute to nature management and supervision along the water, thus making a positive contribution.

206970.explanation: Volt wants to allow more sustainable terrace heating such as electric blankets and encourages entrepreneurs to switch to sustainable alternatives as much as possible. The connecting effect of terraces does not end after the summer.

206971.explanation: Volt does not want to mow the roadside extra because this protects biodiversity. Traffic safety must be maintained, invasive species must be controlled, and native plants are preferred.

206972.explanation: Volt wants Groningen to be climate neutral by 2035. With smart devices, financial incentives, and campaigns, Volt promotes sustainable behavior. Neighborhoods that can become gas-free receive extra support and clear information through the insulation approach and Nij Begun.

206973.explanation: WarmteStad shows that Groningen can jointly own our own heat. This way we get sustainable heat without gas. Profit in one neighborhood helps to make other neighborhoods sustainably affordable.

206974.explanation: Volt wants to help farmers switch to sustainable crops and local food chains. Solar panels, small wind turbines, and manure digesters support this. Cooperative solar parks and multifunctional land use also help make Groningen climate neutral.

206975.explanation: Volt finds forest planting (such as the Meerbos) important to connect nature areas and increase livability, as described in the Dutch Nature Network. This helps plants and animals survive better and keeps nature valuable.

206976.explanation: Volt wants culture to grow and creativity to have space. Early closing of parties like Paradigm actually causes nuisance because people continue their parties elsewhere. Volt therefore wants better consultation with the neighborhood and more measures against noise nuisance.

206977.explanation: For Volt, a good event is about connection, fairness, and sustainability. Therefore, Volt supports sustainable, socially relevant entrepreneurs and cultural organizations that reward talent fairly ('fair pay') and help that talent flourish in Groningen.

206978.explanation: Volt supports permanent safe spaces. This way, every night a safe place and support is offered to everyone who needs it.

206979.explanation: Volt does not want to reduce the number of festivals. We want culture to grow with subsidies, good facilities, and input from creators. This way icons are preserved, creativity has room, and talent thrives in Groningen.

206980.explanation: Volt stands for a modern drug policy based on facts, health, and safety, not fear or prejudice. Volt wants good education, like was done in Utrecht with an educational 'XTC shop'.

206981.explanation: Volt finds sport important for health and social cohesion. Children can already play sports affordably with support from the Youth Sports Fund or the Stadjerspas, but where necessary this can be expanded with an additional contribution so that everyone can participate.

206982.explanation: Volt finds it important that history is presented inclusively. The history of slavery is part of the story of this region and must be visible and acknowledged.

206983.explanation: Volt finds it important that history is presented inclusively. The history of slavery is part of the story of this region and must be visible and acknowledged.

206984.explanation: Volt cherishes and supports local traditions and customs and supports Easter fires and carbide shooting as long as safety, material choice, and location have been properly discussed with the municipality.

206986.explanation: Volt wants to invest in a municipality where everyone can develop from a young age. We make certain museum days, films, and other experiences freely accessible. Volt sees culture as an investment that benefits everyone.

206987.explanation: Volt wants supporters to become co-owners, like the 50+1 model in Germany. This way FC Groningen supporters get a say and more responsibility for their own club. This also prevents undesirable foreign investments.

206988.explanation: The organizations and artists that form the heart of our city must have clarity about their future. Volt supports the new pop venue but also wants to see if other locations are more suitable. This is needed because the Lelylijn might run along the station later.

206989.explanation: Volt focuses on prevention and a strong social foundation. Together with residents and organizations, the party wants to solve problems before they escalate, so that everyone feels safe without violence.

206990.explanation: Volt believes that enforcement officers should reflect the diversity of society. Religious symbols should be included in the clothing policy to promote inclusiveness and connection with all communities.

206991.explanation: Investing in more public order officers (boa’s) is necessary, but a boa is not the right tool in every situation. Volt wants to prevent problems through prevention, a social foundation, and well-designed public spaces. Enough personnel on the streets and appropriate enforcement ensure safety, including at nightlife venues.

206992.explanation: Volt wants camera surveillance only if it truly helps with safety, since cameras often just displace crime without preventing it. Camera surveillance must be necessary, transparent, and privacy must be well protected.

206993.explanation: Volt wants to build 20,000 additional homes in the municipality of Groningen in ten years. We do not want to impose extra rules or conditions on who may live in these homes. We ensure a good mix of affordable rental and owner-occupied housing in all price ranges. This way everyone can find a suitable home.

206994.explanation: Volt wants healthcare workers, teachers, and police to be able to easily rent or buy affordable homes in Groningen, even as supply remains scarce and expensive. This keeps the city accessible for these important professions.

206995.explanation: Volt believes that apartment buildings over six stories are allowed, provided they fit the environment. With greenery, facilities, and residents' input, Volt wants to preserve the identity of each neighborhood and village and create pleasant living places.

206996.explanation: Volt believes that homes should primarily be owner-occupied to prevent vacancies and maintain calm in the housing market. This allows more people to find a house faster and keeps housing fair and accessible for everyone.

206997.explanation: Volt supports an active policy that uses the Vacancy Act extensively. Besides introducing a tax, we support new living arrangements like friends' housing, senior courtyards, and cooperatives, and experiment with repurchase options to get housing faster.

206998.explanation: Volt finds that building aesthetics rules are important for an attractive living climate, as has also become clear in Meerstad. These rules help keep new neighborhoods pleasant and beautiful to live in.

206999.explanation: Finding a room is not a lottery. There are too few and too expensive rooms. Together with housing corporations, schools, and the national government, we will build more affordable student housing nearby. Additionally, we want to learn from countries with successful housing plans, such as Portugal and France.

207000.explanation: Volt wants to build 20,000 extra sustainable and affordable homes in 10 years. Sometimes this may mean building on agricultural land.

207002.explanation: Volt looks to Vienna: The municipality there builds affordable homes, not for profit. We allocate social rental homes and cooperatives across the municipality and give residents a say. This keeps housing fair and livable.

207003.explanation: Volt is based on trust and believes everyone should get a chance to participate through work, education, or other contributions. The municipality should check less and support more, for example by helping people find suitable work.

207004.explanation: Volt believes that people who are motivated and willing to participate should be central, not control. Education, work experience, and income support help residents develop and find work with support from social organizations.

207006.explanation: Volt wants people to be helped respectfully and without prejudice in finding suitable work, with attention to their basic security. If an offered job is not suitable, they must be able to refuse that job.

207007.explanation: Volt supports extra income support and minimum policy for bicycles, devices, or school costs, so that residents can develop without financial worries standing in the way.

207008.explanation: Volt chooses an approach based on trust. By simplifying rules and limiting bureaucracy, problems are solved and social cohesion is maintained.

207009.explanation: Volt wants to prioritize safe, liveable neighborhoods with play and sports facilities. Streets where cars are 'guests' increase safety, climate resilience, and the attractiveness of the neighborhood.

207010.explanation: Volt does not aim to reduce the number of parking spaces, but focuses on livable neighborhoods and alternative transport. Together with the neighborhood and entrepreneurs, we seek the most appropriate solution without widening the gap with the surrounding countryside.

207011.explanation: Revenues from paid parking can be used for cheap and accessible public transport subscriptions. Volt wants to keep parking costs affordable so that the city remains accessible by car.

207012.explanation: Volt believes the city should be accessible to everyone. A walkable street is more important than car parking spaces. We install new bike platforms, improve public transport, and make bike and walking routes safer.

207013.explanation: Volt wants stricter environmental regulations than the national minimum. Abolishing environmental zones does not fit with this. We address pollution and noise and focus additionally on health in vulnerable neighborhoods with a stronger prevention package.

207014.explanation: Volt wants to make roads in Groningen within the built-up area safer by adjusting maximum speeds according to the road design. This prevents accidents and ensures more pleasant, safer, and greener traffic.

207015.explanation: Volt believes sex workers should be broadly supported. Voluntary programs for sex workers who want to stop are good, but sex workers who want to stay in the profession must also have access to care and help, for example with exclusion and discrimination based on their occupation.

207016.explanation: Volt wants to stimulate a green living environment, for example with food gardens. But not every neighborhood needs a food garden. We prefer to leave it up to residents how they want to green their area, for example through Neighborhood Budgets.

206949.explanation: In the past year, partly thanks to GroenLinks-PvdA, a citizens' assembly was organized about waste. This was a success. A citizens' assembly leads to good choices; everyone is heard and trust in democracy increases through it.

206950.explanation: The parliamentary inquiry shows that gas was more important than the people of Groningen. This must really change. A single municipal team can handle earthquake damage faster, better, and more effectively, and homes can be reinforced more quickly and better.

206951.explanation: We choose to invest. With the proceeds from property tax, we pay for things important to our residents. We build bike paths and ensure safe school routes. We provide more housing, organize care, and create many sports and playgrounds and cultural facilities.

206952.explanation: For many, student life is the best time of their lives, but due to housing shortages and performance pressure, it can also be tough. With a student affairs department, we can help the 40,000 students in Groningen even better, especially if students also work here as experiential experts.

206953.explanation: We work together towards a sustainable future. We do this by focusing more on reuse of materials. We also buy locally and sustainably. The municipality sets a good example. This also applies to our social goals.

206954.explanation: Heritage and local traditions are important, but culture is more than that. Culture connects Groningen's past with innovation and influences from outside. Through subsidies for museums, pop stages, amateur arts, and theaters, we want accessible culture for everyone, including people with little money.

206955.explanation: Our Groningen youth can count on the municipality if they need help. Therefore, we should not cut budgets, but invest to reduce waiting lists. Early intervention can prevent much suffering. We want to give caregivers more room and less administrative pressure.

206956.explanation: A begging ban does not solve poverty. It does not help to fine people in vulnerable positions. We focus on help, shelter, and guidance. We choose an approach involving experiential experts instead of a begging ban.

206957.explanation: We invest in both climate and care. Good climate policy is also social policy. In Groningen, we choose a fair energy transition: carbon neutral by 2035, with support for people with less money to make their homes sustainable and lower their energy bills. We do not cut funding for this.

206958.explanation: The phase-out of fossil fuels is necessary for a sustainable Groningen. In our tenders and purchasing, we therefore encourage companies to use as few fossil fuels as possible. Where possible, we only do business with companies that do not use fossil fuels.

206959.explanation: Having a drink on a terrace is fun and creates atmosphere. But money is also earned from terraces on municipal grounds. We ask a fee for that. Besides hospitality terraces, we also ensure there are enough free seating places where you can sit without having to buy anything.

206960.explanation: Advertising tax reduces clutter in public spaces by limiting advertisements or asking a fair, small contribution for them. Currently, most displays and businesses are exempt, and there are extra exemptions for social organizations.

206961.explanation: We invest extra in neighborhoods with many children living in poverty, because sadly where your cradle stands still determines your future. We make Groningen fairer through good education, after-school activities, and meeting places. A Groningen with the very best community centers and greenery.

206962.explanation: The beautiful Groningen nightlife has no closing times, and as far as GroenLinks-PvdA is concerned, it should stay that way. We also want to extend Sunday opening hours. However, small Groningen entrepreneurs without staff, who cannot always open, must be protected.

206963.explanation: Groningen helps people fleeing war and violence. There is no asylum crisis, but a reception crisis due to long procedures and too few homes. We choose humane policy with temporary accommodation so asylum seekers can stay safely and not be left on the street.

206964.explanation: All people in urgent situations in Groningen get priority for social rental housing, such as after a divorce, an unsafe home situation, or other reason. Whether you are a refugee or not. People allowed to stay here must be able to start their life in the Netherlands.

206965.explanation: We want no one to sleep on the streets in Groningen. And in Groningen, no one is illegal. Therefore, we offer help to people in vulnerable positions. People living on the streets cannot think about the future or work on returning.
